front: How do you maintain an Iceberg table — expiring snapshots, removing orphan files?
back: Iceberg tables require routine maintenance to prevent unbounded storage growth. Snapshot expiration removes old snapshots and their associated metadata files, making the data files they exclusively referenced eligible for deletion. In Spark: `SparkActions.get().expireSnapshots(table).expireOlderThan(System.currentTimeMillis() - TimeUnit.DAYS.toMillis(7)).execute()`. This keeps 7 days of history for time travel. Orphan file removal deletes data files that exist on storage but aren't referenced by any snapshot (leftover from failed writes, aborted jobs). In Spark: `SparkActions.get().deleteOrphanFiles(table).olderThan(System.currentTimeMillis() - TimeUnit.DAYS.toMillis(3)).execute()` — the 3-day buffer prevents deleting files from in-progress commits. In Flink SQL: `CALL system.expire_snapshots('db.table', TIMESTAMPADD(DAY, -7, NOW()))`. Schedule both procedures in a daily maintenance job. Don't skip orphan removal — a table with millions of small streaming files plus failed write orphans can accumulate significant storage cost over months.
